name: France
topHurdle: EDF dominance and existing reactor fleet limits SMR deployment windows.
rationale: France is nuclear-first with 70% nuclear generation and strong regulatory framework. EDF partnership or concession model required. High financing support and carbon commitment create opportunity.

nuclearCompanies.name: EDF
nuclearCompanies.notes: Dominates generation and grid; controls deployment sites

name: Germany
topHurdle: Energiewende policy disfavors new nuclear; regulatory hostility and limited licensing pathway.
rationale: Strong grid deregulation and carbon commitment, but nuclear legally limited to existing fleet. Policy shift possible but uncertain; focus on corporate offtakes and export licensing.

nuclearCompanies.name: PreussenElektra
nuclearCompanies.notes: Legacy reactors only; minimal new deployment

partners.name: Corporate offtake partners
partners.notes: Tech and manufacturing sectors (Siemens, SAP, BASF) seeking clean energy

name: Poland
topHurdle: Coal-dependent energy economy and geopolitical tension with Russia create investment hesitation.
rationale: First nuclear program underway (Polskie Elektrownie Jądrowe); regulatory framework emerging and government committed. Financing via EU and US programs. JV with large reactor operators preferred initially.

nuclearCompanies.name: Polskie Elektrownie Jądrowe
nuclearCompanies.notes: State nuclear company; overseeing first large reactors

name: Czech Republic
topHurdle: Existing domestic reactor fleet and limited new build sites; Czech regulatory preference for proven designs.
rationale: Established nuclear program (ČEZ operates Temelín and Dukovany); strong regulatory framework and carbon targets. New units (Dukovany 7-8) in procurement; partnership with utilities and vendors critical.

nuclearCompanies.name: ČEZ
nuclearCompanies.notes: Largest energy company; leads nuclear expansion

name: Slovakia
topHurdle: Limited grid capacity and export focus; energy-intensive industries relocated to EU/Western markets.
rationale: Slovakia operates 4 VVER units (Bohunice, Mochovce); regulatory framework established. Mochovce completion and new build limited by market size; focus on grid optimization and industrial partnerships.

name: Hungary
topHurdle: Pro-Russia geopolitical stance complicates Western partnership and financing.
rationale: Paks nuclear station (4 VVER units) operates; government backing for expansion (Paks II with Russian tech). SMR opportunity via alternative partnerships or grid services; regulatory framework solid.

partners.name: Rosatom/Gazprom (Paks II)
partners.notes: Russian reactor and financing; political channel

partners.name: EU financing institutions
partners.notes: Alternative funding for SMR or diversification

name: Romania
topHurdle: Energy mix still coal-heavy; limited grid upgrade funding and industrial base for nuclear supply chain.
rationale: Cernavoda operates 2 CANDU units; regulatory framework adequate. Government backing for Cernavoda 3-4 completion. Industrial partnerships with EU manufacturers and grid modernization critical.

name: Bulgaria
topHurdle: Coal dominance, post-Fukushima closures, and limited institutional capacity slow nuclear deployment.
rationale: Kozloduy operates 2 VVER units (3 retired post-Fukushima); regulatory framework fragile. Government interest in new build but financing and technical expertise gaps. EU support and vendor partnership essential.

name: Finland
topHurdle: Existing fleet and Olkiluoto 3 capacity sufficient; limited new build demand despite carbon targets.
rationale: World-class regulatory framework and waste management (Posiva repository). Olkiluoto 3 online 2023. Strong carbon and energy independence commitment. Focus on SMR for district heat and industrial process heat.

nuclearCompanies.name: Fortum
nuclearCompanies.notes: Operates Loviisa units; active in grid services

name: Sweden
topHurdle: Existing nuclear provides 40% of generation; capacity sufficiency and grid saturation limit new build.
rationale: 10 operational reactors and world-leading waste management (Forsmark final repository). Policy shift toward new build and lifetime extensions. SMR opportunity for district heating and industrial heat; grid services high-value.

nuclearCompanies.name: Vattenfall
nuclearCompanies.notes: Operates majority of reactors; major grid player

name: Netherlands
topHurdle: Gas dominance in energy; nuclear policy historically uncertain; high land/water constraints for new reactors.
rationale: Petten and Borssele operate; policy shift toward new build and SMR. High electricity prices and carbon targets create offtake demand. Deregulated market favors PPAs; grid operator TenneT critical partner.

name: Belgium
topHurdle: Political divisions on nuclear; lifetime extensions of existing reactors compete with new build for capital and regulatory focus.
rationale: 7 reactors (Doel, Tihange) operating; policy reversed on phase-out (2022); new build and lifetime extensions proceeding. Deregulated market, strong carbon targets, and high prices favor SMR offtakes.

name: United Kingdom
topHurdle: Large-reactor projects (Hinkley C, Sizewell C) dominate industry focus and financing; SMR competes for regulatory and political attention.
rationale: 8 operating reactors; Hinkley C, Sizewell C, and Wylfa in development. Office for Nuclear Regulation (ONR) world-class. Fully deregulated market with strong carbon targets and industrial demand.

nuclearCompanies.name: Rolls-Royce SMR
nuclearCompanies.notes: Leading UK SMR developer; government backing

partners.name: Corporate offtake (BP, Shell, INEOS, Rolls-Royce, GlaxoSmithKline)

name: Estonia
topHurdle: Oil shale energy legacy and geopolitical risk from Russia; limited domestic nuclear expertise and regulatory capacity.
rationale: No operational reactors; oil shale phase-out driving carbon strategy. Regional interest (Baltic Nuclear Forum) but limited institutional capacity. EU financing and foreign vendor critical; grid integration via Nordics.

nuclearCompanies.name: Elering
nuclearCompanies.notes: Grid operator; critical for regional integration

name: Lithuania
topHurdle: Ignalina closure (2009) and political reluctance; geopolitical risk from Russia; limited technical expertise.
rationale: Ignalina decommissioned; renewed interest in small modular reactors. Government support growing but regulatory capacity limited. EU support and regional cooperation (Nordics) essential for deployment.

name: Latvia
topHurdle: Hydropower abundance reduces nuclear urgency; geopolitical risk from Russia; minimal nuclear infrastructure and expertise.
rationale: High hydropower generation (50%+) reduces nuclear demand. Policy interest emerging via Baltic Nuclear Forum but limited capacity. Regional cooperation and EU support drive any potential deployment.

name: Italy
topHurdle: Nuclear phase-out policy (post-Fukushima referenda); political opposition to new reactors; regulatory framework for new build absent.
rationale: High electricity prices and carbon targets create demand. Recent policy thaw and government interest in SMR (2023). Regulatory pathway uncertain; focus on corporate offtakes and pilot projects to shift opinion.

name: Spain
topHurdle: High renewables penetration reducing new nuclear urgency; lifetime extension focus dominates capital and political attention.
rationale: 7 reactors operating; strong regulatory framework (CSN). Renewable energy boom but carbon targets and electricity prices create SMR offtake opportunity. Deregulated market with grid integration via Red Eléctrica.

name: Portugal
topHurdle: No nuclear infrastructure; political opposition to new reactors; regulatory pathway for licensing non-existent.
rationale: High renewable penetration but carbon targets and export demand create potential. Government interest in SMR growing (2024). Regulatory framework development and pilot projects critical for momentum.

name: Greece
topHurdle: Coal legacy and fiscal constraints; no nuclear infrastructure; geopolitical tensions with Turkey complicate regional cooperation.
rationale: Recent policy interest in SMR (2024) signals shift. High electricity prices and carbon targets create demand. Regulatory framework absent; EU financing and international expertise required; grid operator IPTO critical partner.

name: Vietnam
topHurdle: Coal dominance in energy mix; limited nuclear supply chain; Chinese geopolitical influence on reactor selection.
rationale: Coal power phase-out via 2050 carbon commitment. Domestic demand growing rapidly; government backing for nuclear and SMR. Vietnam Electricity (EVN) key partner. China influence via Rosatom financing.

partners.name: Rosatom
partners.notes: Reactor and financing; Chinese pipeline competition

name: Indonesia
topHurdle: Coal dominance and subsidy; limited nuclear expertise and infrastructure; regulatory framework nascent; limited financing capacity.
rationale: Archipelago geography and growing electrification demand. Government interest in SMR for distributed power and desalination. PLN (state utility) control over grid; vendor and financing partner critical.

nuclearCompanies.name: PT PLN
nuclearCompanies.notes: Monopoly on electricity generation and distribution

name: Philippines
topHurdle: High electricity costs and coal dependence; limited nuclear experience (no operating reactors); anti-nuclear sentiment post-1986; regulatory pathway unclear.
rationale: High energy costs and demand growth create incentive. Government policy shift toward nuclear (2022); CHED and DOE coordinating. Meralco (utility) control limits independent power. US and Japan vendor/financing partners preferred.

nuclearCompanies.name: Philippine Nuclear Research Institute
nuclearCompanies.notes: R&D and regulatory role; limited capacity

name: Thailand
topHurdle: Gas-based generation dominance; limited nuclear expertise; regulatory framework underdeveloped; political instability.
rationale: Natural gas dependent (40%+) creates diversification incentive. Government interest in SMR for industry and grid. EGAT (state utility) controls generation; financing partner critical for risk mitigation.

name: Malaysia
topHurdle: Natural gas abundance and cheap coal reduce nuclear urgency; limited nuclear program history; regulatory framework minimal.
rationale: Gas reserves support current generation. Policy interest in SMR for grid reliability and industrial demand growing. TNB (state utility) monopoly; vendor partnership and financing critical.

nuclearCompanies.name: TNB
nuclearCompanies.notes: Dominant utility; controls generation and distribution

name: Bangladesh
topHurdle: Natural gas depletion driving energy transition; limited nuclear expertise and financing; heavy Russian/Chinese technology influence.
rationale: Gas reserves declining; coal and renewable expansion planned. Nuclear interest growing via Russia/China partnerships (Rooppur). BPDB (state utility) control; financing partner and Russian tech preference strong.

nuclearCompanies.name: BPDB
nuclearCompanies.notes: State utility; controls generation and distribution

partners.name: Rosatom
partners.notes: VVER reactor supply and financing for Rooppur

name: South Korea
topHurdle: Limited new build sites domestically; export focus on Middle East and Southeast Asia; geopolitical tension with North Korea.
rationale: 24 operating reactors; world-class APR1400 design. Domestic capacity sufficient; export strategy via Middle East and SE Asia. KEPCO (utility) and KHNP strong partners. Industrial demand for SMR high.

partners.name: Industrial offtake (Samsung, Hyundai, LG, SK Hynix, TSMC foundries)

name: Japan
topHurdle: Post-Fukushima stress test and regulatory burden slow domestic new build; export market focus and industrial heat demand high.
rationale: 29 reactors operational (post-Fukushima restarts); world-class engineering and supply chain. Export strategy via Middle East and SE Asia. High industrial demand (manufacturing, data centers). TEPCO and others strong partners.

partners.name: Industrial offtake (Toyota, Honda, Sony, Panasonic, Nintendo, TSMC, data centers)

name: India
topHurdle: Coal dominance and heavy subsidies; limited nuclear supply chain; India-specific fuel cycle requirements and technology control.
rationale: 22 operating reactors; indigenous technology development. Rapid electricity demand growth. DAE and NTPC partnership strong. Financing challenges but government commitment growing. US/Japan partnerships expanding.

nuclearCompanies.name: NTPC
nuclearCompanies.notes: Largest utility company; nuclear expansion lead

name: Taiwan
topHurdle: Geopolitical risk from China; nuclear phase-out policy (reversed 2022); renewable transition demand competes with nuclear.
rationale: 3 operational reactors; policy reversed on nuclear (2022). High-tech manufacturing demand drives electricity and SMR interest. Taipower (utility) control; US and Japan partnerships strong.

partners.name: Industrial offtake (TSMC, MediaTek, Foxconn, Hon Hai)

name: Singapore
topHurdle: Space constraints prohibit on-island nuclear; regulatory pathway for SMR unclear; regional imports and LNG dominance.
rationale: High electricity costs and carbon targets. Limited land but strong grid interconnection to Malaysia. Policy interest in SMR for industry (refineries, petrochemicals, data centers). SP Group (utility) and regional power critical.

partners.name: Industrial offtake (Shell, ExxonMobil, Jurong Island petrochemicals, data centers)

name: Pakistan
topHurdle: Energy deficit and heavy debt service; China dependency for financing; geopolitical instability with India; limited independent regulation.
rationale: 5 reactors operational; Chinese partnership strong (Karachi K2/K3). Electricity deficit and load shedding chronic. China financing model dominates. Limited alternative vendor access.

nuclearCompanies.name: PAEC
nuclearCompanies.notes: Atomic Energy Commission; operates all reactors

partners.name: China/CNNC
partners.notes: Reactor design, supply, and financing dominance

name: UAE
topHurdle: Oil/gas abundance and low electricity prices reduce nuclear urgency; diversification strategy and COP28 carbon commitment drive demand.
rationale: Barakah 4 units online (2021-2024); Westinghouse partnership strong. Low cost energy reduces urgency but COP28 leadership and diversification policy support expansion. ENEC and EWEC strong partners.

nuclearCompanies.name: ENEC
nuclearCompanies.notes: Nuclear developer; Barakah operator and future expansion

nuclearCompanies.name: FANR
nuclearCompanies.notes: Federal Authority for Nuclear Regulation; world-class

name: Saudi Arabia
topHurdle: Oil abundance and low energy costs; limited nuclear experience; Vision 2030 diversification and energy independence drive nuclear interest.
rationale: No operational reactors; Vision 2030 targets 50 GW renewables by 2030 and nuclear expansion post-2030. High electricity demand growth. King Abdullah City for Atomic and Renewable Energy (KACARE) leads planning.

name: Turkey
topHurdle: Akkuyu project dominance and Russian technology preference; limited alternative vendor acceptance; geopolitical tensions with Greece.
rationale: Akkuyu 4 units (Russian VVER) under construction; first online 2023. Strong Russian and Chinese partnerships. Government backing high; domestic electricity demand growing. TETAŞ (utility) control strong.
